lines.text: The classroom hums with the specific chaos of two weeks into the school year — everyone almost knows where they belong.
Settle down. Trimester project — one week, working model of a scientific principle. A district judge is coming to look at your genius up close, so let's actually have some.
lines.action: taps pen twice on the desk

lines.text: New school, new project, new ways to disappear into the wallpaper. Cool. Love it here already.
Groups are on the board. Find your name, find your people, don't find a reason to complain to me about either.
Chairs scrape. Wren scans the board — finds her name wedged between two others.
Castillo... Okafor... Nassar. A name everyone knows, a name I don't, and me in the middle. Very balanced sandwich.
Castillo, right? You're with me. Good.
lines.emotion: already grinning, turning around in his chair

lines.text: I don't lose.
Cool, cool. No pressure then.
That's the spirit. Kind of.

lines.emotion: barely looking up from her notebook

lines.text: Hi. I'm Wren.
I know. It's on the board.
Right. Yeah.
lines.emotion: laughs half a second too late

lines.text: One week, people. Show me the work, not just the answer.
Library study room, after school. Books unopened. Dez already has something else in mind.
Okay so — don't freak out — but I think I just saved us a week.
lines.action: spins a flash drive on the table

lines.text: That's either really good or really illegal.
Neither. My brother did basically this exact project two years ago. Got an A. Data's all here. We just... update the numbers a little. Nobody's gonna check.
Priya's pen stops tapping. She goes very still.
It's not cheating, it's efficiency. There's a difference — look it up.
Priya says nothing. Her eyes flick to Wren and stay there.
Cool. Cool. Love being the deciding vote nobody asked me to cast.
So? Easy way or hard way?
The flash drive goes back in Dez's pocket. Actual books come out. It's slower. It's also actually theirs.
Okay, real research. Whatever. What are we even measuring?
...I mean — we could do something with plant growth and light color? It's simple, but nobody actually does it right.
...Huh. Okay. That's not bad.
Oh. Um. Cool.
lines.emotion: trying to hide that she's surprised to be heard

lines.text: See, this is way better than stolen data and a guilty conscience.
Don't push it.
Priya sits a little taller. It's a start.
The old file opens easier than it should. Charts, numbers, a whole project already finished two years ago.
See? Look at this. This is basically done. We just gotta make it ours.
It's definitely somebody's.
Details.
...it's fine.
lines.emotion: staring at the screen, chewing on her pen cap — the tone that means it is not fine

lines.text: You good?
This is gonna save us so much time it's actually insane.
It is easier this way. Nobody says out loud why that feels like a problem.
Days later. Whatever path they took, the project's taking shape — and starting to show cracks.
...I mean, it's probably not a big deal, but — what if we simplified the whole design? Fewer variables. It'd actually be more interesting to present, I think.
That'll take too long. We're doing it my way.
Priya goes very still. Shrinks back into her chair. The pen tapping stops mid-rhythm.
We've got five days. Not five weeks.
There it is again. Same move, different day. Guess it's my turn to decide if I'm watching or doing something about it.
Wait — actually, I think Priya's idea is better. Fewer variables means less that can go wrong presenting it live.
...I mean — okay. I didn't say it was bad, I said —
You said 'my way.' That's not the same as 'the better way.'
...Fine. Fine. We'll try it her way. If it tanks, I'm saying I told you so.
...it won't tank.
lines.emotion: sits up straighter than all week, quick sharp glance at Wren, then away

lines.text: Wren doesn't say anything. The moment passes like it was never going to go any other way.
Okay. My way it is. Moving on.
Priya doesn't argue. She also doesn't really talk again for the rest of the session — not sulking. Just gone somewhere quieter.
Nobody else notices. That's almost the worst part.
Outside the library, Wren catches up before Priya disappears toward the buses.
Hey — Priya. Wait up a sec.
...hey.
Your idea, earlier. The simpler design. That was actually good. Better than what we're doing.
...you didn't say that in there.
lines.emotion: sizing up if this is real

lines.text: ...yeah. I know. I'm saying it now.
...it's fine. You don't have to—
I'm not saying it because I have to.
...okay.
lines.emotion: almost a smile, still braced, but a little less

lines.text: Video call, kitchen table, project due tomorrow morning. On screen, three tired faces and one botched data set.
Okay, so trial four's a disaster, we're missing half of trial two, and the judge is here in —
—way less time than I want to think about.
...we could just rerun trial four. It's not that late.
It's ten PM on a school night, Priya. We are not 'just rerunning' anything.
So what's the move?
We smooth it. Fill in what the numbers should've been if trial four hadn't glitched. It's basically the same thing, just cleaner.
Priya's pen taps out its specific, careful rhythm against her sketchbook.
Nobody's gonna know the difference. We know what the pattern is. We're just... finishing it early.
The clock on Wren's kitchen wall is suddenly the loudest thing in the room.
Ten fifteen. Ten thirty. They're still on the call, actually rerunning trial four together, camera propped against a water glass.
I hate this. I hate this so much. Why is science like this.
Science is just homework that fights back.
Wait — did Priya just laugh? Somebody clip that.
...shut up.
Ten forty-five. Priya suddenly sits forward.
Wait. Wait — the thermometer's reading room temp, not the actual sample. That's the whole error. It's not the experiment, it's the tool.
...Priya. Priya. You just saved the entire project.
...I mean. Yeah. I did.
There she is.
It takes twenty minutes. The spreadsheet fills in clean, expected, exactly what a judge would want to see.
See? Told you. Efficient.
Efficient.

lines.emotion: quiet, staring at the numbers like they might argue back

lines.text: Okay. We're done. Everybody get some sleep.
The call ends. Nobody says goodnight the normal way — no jokes, no 'see you tomorrow.' Just three clicks, one after another, and silence.
Presentation day. The district judge sits in the back row with a clipboard and an unreadable face. Ms. Alvarez circles the room, pen tapping twice at their table.
These results are unusually clean.
Walk me through how you got this measurement.
Three sets of eyes land on Wren, without meaning to.
New school, new me. Except apparently the me who has to answer this.
Our timeline got tight — one of our trials didn't run clean, so we had to rework some of the data to finish on time. It's not perfect. I wanted to say that up front.
...I appreciate you telling me that instead of me finding it myself.
You'll present with a noted caveat. That's not nothing.
It's not a perfect score. Dez looks a little pale. Priya looks... relieved.
Nobody in this room is in trouble for telling me the truth late. They're in trouble for telling me a story instead.
That was, um — that was Priya's part of the data set. I think there might've been an error somewhere in there.
Priya's face falls in front of the whole room — quick, then carefully blank.
...I can check it again.
...Do that.
Dez stays very quiet. Visibly relieved it landed somewhere else.
There was a mix-up with one of the trials near the end — we all kind of scrambled to fix it last minute. It's on all of us, honestly.
...Walk me through it sometime this week. In detail.
The judge moves on to the next table. Nobody's humiliated. Nobody's fully off the hook, either.
That's not a lie. It's also not the whole truth. Feels like standing on a step that isn't quite there.
Same direction, same sidewalk, same silence that's been sitting since third period.
...you didn't have to say anything in there. Earlier.
I know.
A block passes. Just footsteps.
...it's fine.
...Priya.
lines.emotion: catching the tone, the one that means it's not fine

lines.text: ...what.
I should've said something sooner. Not just today — the other stuff too. With Dez.
...you don't have to—

I'm not doing the thing where I say sorry and then don't change anything. New rule, for next time: no faking results, no talking over each other. I'll actually enforce it. Badly, probably, but I'll try.
...you'll 'badly enforce' it.
Aggressively. Very aggressively.
...okay. Deal.
Wren opens her mouth. Closes it. The moment passes like a bus she didn't run for.
...so, um. You have a good weekend planned?
...not really. You?
Not really.
They talk about nothing the rest of the way. It's easier. It's also quieter than it should be.
Cool. Cool. Very normal amount of nothing being said right now.
Wren catches Dez by the bike rack before he can disappear into his afternoon.
Hey. Can I say something and you not turn it into a joke for once?
...that's a lot to ask, but go ahead.
The way you talk over Priya — cutting her off, deciding for everyone, all of it. It's not okay. And today, letting it land on her instead of you — that wasn't okay either.
I didn't 'let' anything, you're the one who—
...yeah. Okay. That wasn't — I shouldn't have talked to her like that. Whatever. It's not a big deal.
It kind of is, though.

lines.emotion: doesn't argue this time, just nods once, looking somewhere else

options.text: That's basically cheating. Let's do our own.
I mean... it could save us time. Let's just look.

a.text: ...Fine. Fine! We'll do it the boring way first, see how far that gets us.

a.emotion: quiet exhale, like she'd been holding it

b.text: See, this is why you're my favorite so far.

b.emotion: doesn't say anything, just watches the screen

options.text: Back Priya's idea out loud, right now.
Stay quiet and let Dez keep leading.
Say nothing now, but check in with Priya after.

b.text: The moment passes. Dez keeps talking like it already settled itself.

c.text: Not now. Later. I'll say something later.

options.text: Let's just redo it right, even if it takes longer.
Fine — let's just clean it up and submit.

a.text: ...Fine. FINE. But if we're up till midnight, you're buying breakfast.

b.text: See, this is why I like working with you.

options.text: Admit the timeline was tight and be honest about what happened.
Imply it was Priya's part of the work that went wrong.
Give a vague, half-true answer that protects everyone a little.

c.action: taps pen twice, unconvinced but moving on

options.text: Apologize to Priya and suggest real ground rules going forward.
Let it go and talk about something else.
Find Dez and tell him directly the pressure wasn't okay.

b.text: The block keeps going. So does the quiet.

c.text: Priya's already gone, headed the other way. This one's for Dez.

lines.text: Telling Ms. Alvarez the truth cost us half a letter grade. Standing here, it doesn't feel like it cost us anything at all.
For what it's worth — I'd rather have the caveat and the truth than the clean grade and the guilt.
...yeah. Same, actually.
...you've had my back a lot this week. I noticed.
Yeah, well. Somebody had to be annoying about it.
...you're not that annoying.
Give it time.
Priya's part of the data was never actually wrong. I know that. She knows I know that.
...see you tomorrow, I guess.
...yeah. See you.
That's not nothing. It's also not enough.
Nothing bad happened today. That's not the same as something good happening.
...it's fine, really.
...yeah. I know.
I keep telling myself that's an answer.
...you tell everybody the truth whether they want it or not, huh.
Pretty much. Ask Ms. Alvarez.
...yeah. I heard about that.
I've been picking Priya's side all week. Might as well finish the job.
...she tell you to say this?
No. She doesn't know I'm saying it. That's kind of the point.
...noted.

voiceNotes: Wren: fast and joke-armored when nervous, slower and blunter when something actually matters — the shift between s2-3 (quip) and s11a-3 (plain, no hedge) marks growth. Dez: outcome-language ('we win,' 'efficiency'), leans back when confident, cracks knuckles right before he says something he half-knows is wrong, and gets quiet instead of loud when actually rattled (s11c-6). Priya: hedges ('I mean, it's probably not a big deal') until she trusts the room, drops it entirely once she does (s7a-8, s11a-4 — no hedge left). Her 'it's fine' is a tell, not a fact, every time. Ms. Alvarez never says 'you're lying' — she asks a question she already knows the answer to and lets the silence do the accusing.

alternatives: You said 'my way.' Since when is that the same as 'the right way'?
alternativeLines.reasoning: Slightly sharper alternative if the tone should read more confrontational than dry; current version keeps Wren's dry-understated register intact.

alternatives: Next time we make an actual rule — no faking numbers, no talking over each other. I mean it.
I want an actual rule this time. No fake data, nobody talked over. Deal?
alternativeLines.reasoning: Current version keeps the half-joking hedge ('badly, probably') that fits Wren's defusing-humor pattern while still landing the sincerity; alternatives are more direct if the scene needs less deflection.

alternatives: There was some confusion with a trial near the end — we fixed what we could in time.
One of our trials ran into problems and we adjusted the numbers to compensate.
alternativeLines.reasoning: Keeps the half-true quality without tipping into either full confession or outright blame-shift; wording can be tuned for how evasive vs. technical it should sound.
